---
title: "Examples of masking data"
author: "Ana Martinovici"
date: "Last compiled on `r format(Sys.time(), '%Y-%m-%d at %H:%M:%S')`"
output: 
    bookdown::html_document2:
        toc: true
        toc_float: 
            collapsed: false
        number_sections: true
        code_folding: show
        theme: cerulean
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

This file provides examples of how to work with data that is not shared in the repo, and that can not be shared as-is with others.

```{r}
library("tidyverse")

# data is read from an external location (i.e., not in the repo)
path_to_data <- here::here(Sys.getenv("PATH_TO_DROPBOX"), 
						   "Teaching",
						   "RSM",
						   "MM_programme",
						   "DFCP",
						   "DFCP_2025-2026",
						   "Spotify_data")
```

I have placed a data access request for my Spotify data and received in return 3 archives. After unzipping the archives, these are the files within each folder:

```{r}

printtree::print_rtree(path = here::here(path_to_data))


```

The sheer number of files and the amount of information within each of them is overwhelming. For each of the 3 archives / groups of data files, there is ReadMe file that provides additional information. These ReadMe files are pdfs and have pages upon pages of tables with `item name` and `item description`. It is possible to go through all of those pages and figure out what each file and variable name stores. However, it is not feasible to do so within a reasonable time, by an average user. For example, the Granular Description of Technical Log Files is about 500 pages long. Data dictionaries would be much more useful if provided in a format that can more easily be processed and stored together with the data it describes.

These files contain personal data and information that I want to keep private. 

For example, `technical_log\Spotify Technical Log Information\recipients.json` contains information about who might have received my data. See screenshot from the ReadMe file:

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(path = here::here("data_outside_repo", "readme_tech_1.png"),
						rel_path = FALSE,
						dpi = 10)
```

However, I can mask some of the data and share the masked version. In doing so, I have in mind the principle of data minimization. That is, I will try to share as little data as necessary for my specific goal. For example, if I want to make a statement about how many companies have received my data, I can mask all company names and share that. The masked data can still be used to count the number of companies - their specific names are not relevant. 

```{r}
orig_recipients <- jsonlite::fromJSON(txt = here::here(path_to_data,
													   "technical_log",
													   "Spotify Technical Log Information",
													   "recipients.json"),
									  simplifyDataFrame = FALSE)
```

This reads in a list with one item:

```{r}

names(orig_recipients)

```

Recipients are split in two types:

```{r}
purrr::map_chr(orig_recipients[["recipients"]], "name")
```

# Example 1

If I want to report the total number of recipients, without the split by type, then I can put the members info in a `df` and then mask their names:

```{r}
df_orig <- orig_recipients[["recipients"]] %>%
	map_dfr(\(x) {
		tibble(
			members = x[["members"]]
		)
	})
```

I will first check how many unique values there are:

```{r}
df_orig %>%
	distinct(members) %>%
	nrow()
```

Good, as many as the number of observations. I can replace the values of the `members` variable with a value that clearly communicates this is masked information.

```{r}

df_processed <- df_orig %>%
	mutate(masked_members = paste0("Masked_Name", row_number())) %>%
	select(-members)

```

Now, `df_processed` can be saved in the repo and shared with others.

# Example 2

If I want to report the number of members by type, then I need to keep that information in.

```{r}

df_orig <- orig_recipients[["recipients"]] %>%
	map_dfr(\(x) {
		tibble(
			name = x[["name"]],
			members = x[["members"]]
		)
	})

df_processed <- df_orig %>%
	group_by(name) %>%
	mutate(masked_members = paste0("Masked_Name", row_number())) %>%
	ungroup() %>%
	select(-members)

```

Now, `df_processed` can be saved in the repo and shared with others.

```{r}
save(df_processed,
	 file = here::here("data_outside_repo",
	 				  "masked_members.RData"))
```
